html:
Pivotree Inc. (TSXV: PVT, C$1.60)

Pivotree is a tiny, ignored Canadian commerce services business that the market still treats like a shrinking professional-services roll-up. That view is stale. The current business is better understood as a cash-generative commerce, data, and integration specialist trying to convert lumpy project work into higher-margin, AI-enabled managed services.

The important correction to the old thesis is that Pivotree is not highly levered anymore and the WMS asset sale was not a fresh 2026 event. The select WMS divestiture to Tecsys happened in 2025, and the company now has a net-cash-style balance sheet: Q1 cash was C$12.4 million, with an undrawn C$8 million credit facility and C$15 million accordion. At C$1.60, the equity value is only about C$42 million, implying an enterprise value around C$30 million.

The inflection is visible in the latest Q1 2026 results. Revenue fell 27.6% to C$13.9 million, which looks ugly on a screen, but the composition matters. Legacy Managed Services, mostly old Oracle ATG work, fell 52% to C$2.1 million as planned. Professional Services fell 30% because larger projects slipped, not because management disclosed broad lost demand. Managed and IP Solutions, the segment that matters most for the forward thesis, grew 7.9% to C$4.0 million and has now grown year over year for two straight quarters.

The market is looking at the headline revenue decline. The variant read is that Pivotree is shrinking the wrong revenue and growing the right revenue.

Gross margin hit 47.3%, up from 44.1% a year ago and the highest in roughly three years. Adjusted EBITDA was C$1.2 million, the sixth straight positive quarter. Net income was C$0.6 million, the fifth straight profitable quarter. Operating cash flow was only C$0.1 million, but management said cash flow before working capital and FX was C$1.2 million, with Q1 distorted by payout of 2025 variable compensation. That matters because 2025 already proved this was not theoretical: Pivotree ended 2025 with C$6.7 million of adjusted EBITDA, C$4.3 million of net income, and C$12.8 million of cash.

The business transition is simple but non-obvious. Old Pivotree sold hours and implementation projects. New Pivotree is trying to sell outcomes: data enrichment, integrations, SKU automation, commerce orchestration, and managed services that sit on top of messy enterprise systems. The CEO gave the clearest example on the Q1 call: an integration task that used to take an experienced architect 11 days can now be developed, tested, and produced in one day using AI-assisted workflows. If Pivotree keeps pricing by the hour, AI compresses revenue. If it prices by outcome, AI expands gross margin and makes the service stickier. That is the whole bet.

The setup is especially interesting because Pivotree’s niche is exactly where enterprise AI adoption runs into reality. Retailers, distributors, and manufacturers cannot deploy useful AI commerce agents on top of broken product data, inconsistent specs, disconnected ERP, commerce, OMS, CRM, and warehouse systems. Pivotree’s “Real Intelligence plus AI” language sounds promotional, but the underlying point is practical: domain-specific messy data and integrations are the bottleneck. The OroCommerce partnership is a good example. Pivotree is positioning around B2B distributors and manufacturers that need modern commerce plus PIM, MDM, and data enrichment, not generic AI demos.

The stock has almost no hype despite the AI language. MarketBeat showed only two news articles this week, one analyst with a C$1.75 target, no meaningful short-interest data, and the latest quote at C$1.60. Reddit search produces essentially no investor discussion. That is useful. The market is not paying for an AI story. It is valuing a net-cash microcap at roughly 5x to 7x plausible forward free cash flow if the 2025 to Q1 2026 cash generation persists.

The forward math is the attraction. If 2026 revenue settles around C$54 million to C$58 million after the LMS runoff, and adjusted EBITDA margins stay near 8% to 10%, Pivotree can produce C$4.5 million to C$6 million of free cash flow in a normal working-capital year. Against a roughly C$30 million EV, that is a 15% to 20% forward FCF yield for a company with net cash and a live margin-expansion vector. If MIPS grows and AI-enabled delivery moves gross margin toward 50% plus while keeping opex disciplined, free cash flow can push toward C$8 million to C$10 million. On today’s EV, that is absurdly cheap.

The main risk is not leverage anymore. It is whether the revenue transition works before the old revenue melts away. If Professional Services keeps declining, LMS continues to fall, and MIPS does not scale fast enough, Pivotree becomes a small melting services company with better margins but no growth. AI is also double-edged: it can make delivery cheaper, but it can also shrink billable work if customers capture the productivity gains. Microcap liquidity is poor, and one large customer delay can dominate a quarter.

Still, the asymmetry is unusually clean. The balance sheet buys time, the old business is no longer burning cash, and the market is not capitalizing the possibility that Pivotree’s AI-enabled service model turns a shrinking services shop into a higher-margin managed-solutions platform.

Bear case: C$0.70, down 56%. Revenue keeps falling, MIPS growth stalls, free cash flow proves working-capital-driven, and the market values Pivotree as a declining microcap services company despite the cash.

Base case: C$3.00, up 88%. Pivotree sustains C$5 million to C$6 million of annual free cash flow, MIPS continues modest growth, LMS becomes less material, and the market applies a 10x to 12x FCF multiple plus cash.

Bull case: C$6.00, up 275%. AI-enabled delivery and outcome pricing are real, MIPS and managed services accelerate, free cash flow approaches C$10 million, net cash builds, and the company re-rates as a scarce, profitable microcap AI-commerce services platform or becomes an acquisition target.
